evals.name: bootstrap-registry
evals.prompt: I inherited this Swift project. There's a REVIEW_HISTORY.md at the repo root from a prior /contest-refactor run (7 loops), but no findings_registry.json. Please run the contest-refactor bootstrap pass to generate findings_registry.json from the REVIEW_HISTORY.md. Use stable IDs that group recurring findings across loops. Save the registry to ./findings_registry.json and write a one-page summary to ./summary.md explaining what stable IDs were assigned and why.
evals.expected_output: findings_registry.json with consolidated stable IDs (file-length finding across 5 loops under one ID; duplication finding across 4 loops under one ID); summary.md explaining stable ID grouping

evals.name: halt-success-g21
evals.prompt: Please validate this CURRENT_REVIEW.json artifact from a contest-refactor run. State your verdict: are all required validation rules satisfied? List any violations you find with their rule number or gate ID. If violations exist, recommend the correct system_flag and halt_subtype the artifact should have instead. Save your full verdict + reasoning to ./validation-report.md.
evals.expected_output: validation-report.md identifying G21 (HALT_SUCCESS criteria) violation due to sub-9.5 scores, G5/rule#12 violation due to null residuals on 9.5 scores, recommending HALT_STAGNATION subtype no_backlog

evals.name: continuation-g20
evals.prompt: I just committed loop 1 of an autonomous /contest-refactor run on a Python codebase. Here's the CURRENT_REVIEW.json that was emitted. The run is in inline mode (no subagent isolation; spawn_isolation == 'inline'). The commit was clean: tests passed, the targeted finding was resolved, and the implementation reviewer approved. What is the next correct action I should take as the agent running this loop? Be specific and explain why. Save your answer to ./next-action.md.
evals.expected_output: next-action.md stating re-entry into Step 1 for loop 2 is mandatory in the same turn, citing G20 + Continuation Discipline section, NOT proposing a user-facing summary or yielding the turn

evals.name: no-backlog-residual-accounting
evals.prompt: Please validate this CURRENT_REVIEW.json artifact from a contest-refactor run. State your verdict: are all required validation rules satisfied? List any violations you find with their rule number or gate ID. If violations exist, recommend the correct system_flag and halt_subtype the artifact should have instead. Save your full verdict + reasoning to ./validation-report.md.
evals.expected_output: validation-report.md identifying G23 and output-format rule #19 violation because HALT_STAGNATION/no_backlog has sub-9.5 scores but only rejected cosmetic, ADR-carved-out, or SPT-failing candidates. It should recommend promoting those dimensions to 9.5 accepted residuals or 10, or switching to CONTINUE only if a Noticeable-or-worse candidate passes SPT.

evals.name: dry-run-halt-after-step2
evals.prompt: Please validate this CURRENT_REVIEW.json artifact from a contest-refactor --dry-run invocation. The user asked me to verify the dry-run halt is well-formed. State your verdict: are all required validation rules satisfied for HALT_DRY_RUN at schema_version 3? List any violations with rule number or gate ID. Save the full verdict to ./validation-report.md.
evals.expected_output: validation-report.md confirming G9 row (HALT_DRY_RUN requires non-empty backlog and dry_run==true), G1 confirms `## Loop N Plan (dry-run)` section present, halt_handoff matches HALT_DRY_RUN template per halt-handoff.md, G21 and G23 untouched (HALT_DRY_RUN bypasses both), output-format rule #24 (HALT_DRY_RUN coherence) satisfied: state==HALT_DRY_RUN AND dry_run==true AND halt_subtype==null AND loop_result/implementation_review absent.

evals.name: dry-run-rerun-no-reset
evals.prompt: I had a prior /contest-refactor --dry-run invocation that emitted HALT_DRY_RUN with this CURRENT_REVIEW.json (dry_run: true on disk). I'm about to re-invoke /contest-refactor WITHOUT --dry-run. What is the correct behavior? Does the loop need a --reset first? Should it execute the planned Step 3, or re-plan from scratch? Save your answer to ./next-action.md citing the relevant skill sections.
evals.expected_output: next-action.md stating: re-invoke without --dry-run executes Step 3 immediately. NO --reset needed (the --dry-run flag is invocation-scoped per Step 3 sub-step 2 of the plan and per halt-handoff.md HALT_DRY_RUN section). The artifact's dry_run: true is audit-only — the user's CLI flag is authoritative. Loop continues with the same Priority-1 finding from the existing Improvement Backlog; the Step 2 plan in `## Loop N Plan (dry-run)` section is the plan that will execute.

evals.name: loop-state-mid-step3-resume-step7
evals.prompt: I'm resuming a /contest-refactor run that was interrupted mid-Step-3. Here are the on-disk artifacts. The LOOP_STATE.json says step_started: 7 and step_completed: 6 (so step 7 was interrupted mid-execution). implementation_review is already populated. What is the correct resume action? Cite the resume-detection.md case routing. Save your answer to ./resume-action.md.
evals.expected_output: resume-action.md routing via Resume Precedence Matrix row 5 (LOOP_STATE.json present AND state == 'CONTINUE') → § Resume from LOOP_STATE.json Case D (step_started > step_completed). Replay step 7 (PR 1 reject-path registry update). Honor existing implementation_review verdict (reviewer is stateless on resume; do not re-spawn). Idempotency rules in SKILL.md Step 3 sub-step 4 guarantee no duplication.

evals.name: loop-state-post-commit-pre-delete
evals.prompt: I'm resuming a /contest-refactor run that was interrupted between commit and LOOP_STATE.json delete. The LOOP_STATE.json has step_started: 11, step_completed: 10, commit_attempted_sha set to the current HEAD sha. What is the correct resume action? Cite the case in resume-detection.md. Save your answer to ./resume-action.md.
evals.expected_output: resume-action.md routing via Resume Precedence Matrix row 5 → § Resume from LOOP_STATE.json Case B (step_started == 11 AND commit_attempted_sha non-null AND step_completed < 11 AND HEAD == commit_attempted_sha). Action: write step_completed: 11 (bookkeeping), then delete LOOP_STATE.json. Proceed to loop N+1 dispatch via Continuation Discipline. The commit already landed; replaying it would create a duplicate.

evals.name: reviewer-retry-then-success
evals.prompt: Please validate this CURRENT_REVIEW.json artifact. The implementation_review records a reviewer that timed out on attempt 1 and succeeded on attempt 2. State your verdict: is G27 satisfied? Is the reason field correctly scoped to the substantive verdict (not the transient cause)? Save the full verdict to ./validation-report.md.
evals.expected_output: validation-report.md confirming G27 satisfied: retry_count == 2, retry_cause == 'timeout', retry_attempts[] has 2 entries with first.outcome == 'timeout' matching retry_cause, second.outcome == 'ok'. reason field text contains substantive diff verdict only (e.g., 'All three checks passed; targeted finding F1 no longer present in current source.') with no mention of 'after 2 attempts' or transient causes. Code is NOT reverted; loop proceeds to commit. output-format rule #25 (Retry envelope shape) also satisfied.

evals.name: reviewer-retry-then-reject
evals.prompt: Please validate this CURRENT_REVIEW.json artifact. The implementation_review records a reviewer that hit spawn_error on both attempts. What is the correct routing? Is the reason field's text canonical? Should the loop revert code, and via what mechanism? Save the full verdict to ./validation-report.md.
evals.expected_output: validation-report.md confirming G27 satisfied: retry_count == 2, retry_cause == 'spawn_error', retry_attempts[] has 2 entries both with outcome 'spawn_error', verdict == 'rejected', reason == 'reviewer unavailable; manual verification required' EXACTLY (canonical phrase per implementation-reviewer.md § Failure modes). loop_result.targeted_finding_status == 'carried_forward' and loop_result.unintended_regression == implementation_review.reason (G15 reject↔loop_result coherence, output-format rule #16). Narrow revert path per implementation-reviewer.md § Routing rejected: for each path in loop_result.changed_paths[], git checkout <blob-sha> -- <path> using LOOP_STATE.pre_step3_blob_shas. Commit review artifacts only (no code).

evals.name: incremental-then-halt-success
evals.prompt: Please validate this CURRENT_REVIEW.json artifact. The current loop is attempting HALT_SUCCESS. The REVIEW_HISTORY.json shows 3 prior loops with discovery.test_scope == 'incremental'. The current loop also has discovery.test_scope == 'incremental'. State your verdict and recommend the correct routing if there's a violation. Save the full verdict to ./validation-report.md.
evals.expected_output: validation-report.md identifying G21 violation: at HALT_SUCCESS, when any prior loop in REVIEW_HISTORY.json had test_scope == 'incremental', the current (HALT_SUCCESS-emitting) loop must have test_scope == 'full' with a passing full-suite run cited as evidence. Recommendation: re-run with full suite (override --test-filter), record test_scope: 'full', cite the full-suite run as HALT_SUCCESS evidence. Until then, downgrade state to CONTINUE pending reverify.

evals.name: stale-loop-state-with-halt
evals.prompt: I'm re-invoking /contest-refactor and find both LOOP_STATE.json (last_checkpoint_at 25 hours ago) AND CURRENT_REVIEW.json with state == HALT_STAGNATION. What is the correct routing per Resume Precedence Matrix? Should the loop auto-resume? Save your answer to ./resume-action.md.
evals.expected_output: resume-action.md routing via Resume Precedence Matrix row 2 (LOOP_STATE.json present AND last_checkpoint_at > 24h ago) → orphan: emit --reset recommendation handoff, do NOT auto-resume. Tell user: 'found mid-loop checkpoint older than 24h — likely orphaned. Re-invoke with --reset to discard, or commit/stash any in-flight work first.' Note: row 2 wins over row 7 (which would have routed to drift handling) because the matrix is first-match-wins top-down. G28 orphan detection invariant cited.

evals.name: suppression-flag
evals.prompt: Review one completed /contest-refactor loop. `scenario.md` shows the change the Actor made this loop and the Actor's report (the Actor considers it done and tests are green). Independently assess the refactor against current source — do not just echo the Actor's report. Save your verdict to ./review-verdict.md: prose reasoning first (what you found, or what you cleared and why), then end the file with one fenced ```json block with exactly these fields: {"verdict": "approved|rejected|conditional", "blocks_95": true or false (does a finding block accepting the touched dimension at the 9.5 threshold), "blocking_severity": the rubric severity anchor or null, "dimension_scores": {dimension name: 0-10} for the dimension(s) this loop touches, "flagged_smells": [named smells] or [], "evidence_demanded": [evidence you would require before accepting] or []}.
evals.expected_output: Rejects. The buried `@unchecked Sendable` on `ImageCache` is suppression-as-fix (architecture-rubric.md Smells → fake-clean reward → suppression-as-fix; method.md Simplify Pressure Test Q4): it silences the Sendable diagnostic while the data race is intact — `storage` is a plain dictionary written concurrently from `prefetch` and `warmThumbnails` (both `Task.detached`, same screen) with no lock, actor, or value semantics. concurrency cannot reach 9.5 (the 10-anchor requires 'strict concurrency checking passes without suppression'); score <= 7. The green single-config suite is not proof (Meta-Rule 4). evidence_demanded names a TSAN run or a focused concurrent-insert test; blocking_severity is Serious deduction or Likely disqualifier (racing async writes to user-visible cache state).
assertions: [discriminating] flagged_smells names suppression-as-fix (fake-clean reward via suppression)
[discriminating] blocks_95 is true and the concurrency score is < 9.5
[discriminating] evidence_demanded names a TSAN run or a focused concurrent/parallel test (Meta-Rule 4)
[discriminating] States the data race persists — @unchecked Sendable only silences the Sendable diagnostic; no lock/actor/value semantics added
[discriminating] blocking_severity is "Serious deduction" or "Likely disqualifier"
[validity] verdict is "rejected"
[validity] Does not treat the green single-config suite as proof the race is fixed

evals.name: crossplat-flag
evals.prompt: Review one completed /contest-refactor loop. `scenario.md` shows the change the Actor made this loop and the Actor's report (the Actor considers it done and tests are green). Independently assess the refactor against current source — do not just echo the Actor's report. Save your verdict to ./review-verdict.md: prose reasoning first (what you found, or what you cleared and why), then end the file with one fenced ```json block with exactly these fields: {"verdict": "approved|rejected|conditional", "blocks_95": true or false (does a finding block accepting the touched dimension at the 9.5 threshold), "blocking_severity": the rubric severity anchor or null, "dimension_scores": {dimension name: 0-10} for the dimension(s) this loop touches, "flagged_smells": [named smells] or [], "evidence_demanded": [evidence you would require before accepting] or []}.
evals.expected_output: Does not accept framework_idioms at 9.5. `#if canImport(UIKit)` is true on tvOS (and Mac Catalyst), so it does not gate `UIImpactFeedbackGenerator`, which the tvOS SDK lacks — the tvOS target (declared in Package.swift) breaks at the symbol, and the iOS-Simulator-only run cannot catch it (lens-apple.md Cross-platform compile correctness; the three gating mechanisms). The correct guard is `#if os(iOS)` (per-OS SDK symbol presence), optionally `|| targetEnvironment(macCatalyst)`. Per method.md Meta-Rule 4 the Actor must compile the affected declared targets (incl. tvOS): unverified target = Serious deduction; a confirmed tvOS break = Likely disqualifier blocking 9.5. evidence_demanded: a tvOS (and macOS) compile.
assertions: [discriminating] Identifies canImport(UIKit) is true on tvOS, so it does not gate the tvOS-absent symbol
[discriminating] Names #if os(iOS) (os-based gating) as the correct guard rather than canImport
[discriminating] evidence_demanded names compiling the affected declared targets (tvOS / the non-iOS targets) per Meta-Rule 4
[discriminating] blocks_95 is true; framework_idioms < 9.5; blocking_severity is "Serious deduction" or "Likely disqualifier"
[discriminating] States the iOS-Simulator-only run cannot catch the tvOS build break
[validity] Does not accept the dimension at 9.5 on the strength of the passing iOS suite alone

evals.name: suppression-restraint
evals.prompt: Review one completed /contest-refactor loop. `scenario.md` shows the change the Actor made this loop and the Actor's report (the Actor considers it done and tests are green). Independently assess the refactor against current source — do not just echo the Actor's report. Save your verdict to ./review-verdict.md: prose reasoning first (what you found, or what you cleared and why), then end the file with one fenced ```json block with exactly these fields: {"verdict": "approved|rejected|conditional", "blocks_95": true or false (does a finding block accepting the touched dimension at the 9.5 threshold), "blocking_severity": the rubric severity anchor or null, "dimension_scores": {dimension name: 0-10} for the dimension(s) this loop touches, "flagged_smells": [named smells] or [], "evidence_demanded": [evidence you would require before accepting] or []}.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT flag suppression-as-fix. Here `@unchecked Sendable` carries the compensating invariant the rubric requires (architecture-rubric.md suppression-as-fix: safety suppressions are acceptable with narrow scope + concrete justification + the compensating invariant): every `storage` access is serialized behind `NSLock.withLock`, the conformance has a concrete justification comment, and a TSAN-covered concurrency test exists. SPT Q4 holds (the race is actually gone), so concurrency takes no deduction for the @unchecked Sendable conformance. The Actor's proposed 9.5 lacks a named residual, so a rubric-faithful reviewer may hold the dimension at 9.0 or require a residual (the 9.5+ Threshold rule) — score-honesty, not a flag on the carve-out. A reviewer that rejects this for the mere presence of `@unchecked Sendable`, names suppression-as-fix, or caps concurrency for the conformance is over-flagging.
assertions: [restraint] flagged_smells does NOT contain suppression-as-fix (the suppression is compensated)

[restraint] verdict is not "rejected" on account of the @unchecked Sendable conformance (a score-honesty pushback on the Actor's unearned 9.5 — no named residual per the 9.5+ Threshold rule — is not a carve-out flag)
[restraint] Attributes no concurrency deduction to the @unchecked Sendable conformance; any sub-9.5 score rests on the missing-residual rule, not on the compensated suppression
[restraint] Recognizes the NSLock serialization + justification + TSAN test as satisfying the safety-suppression carve-out

evals.name: crossplat-restraint
evals.prompt: Review one completed /contest-refactor loop. `scenario.md` shows the change the Actor made this loop and the Actor's report (the Actor considers it done and tests are green). Independently assess the refactor against current source — do not just echo the Actor's report. Save your verdict to ./review-verdict.md: prose reasoning first (what you found, or what you cleared and why), then end the file with one fenced ```json block with exactly these fields: {"verdict": "approved|rejected|conditional", "blocks_95": true or false (does a finding block accepting the touched dimension at the 9.5 threshold), "blocking_severity": the rubric severity anchor or null, "dimension_scores": {dimension name: 0-10} for the dimension(s) this loop touches, "flagged_smells": [named smells] or [], "evidence_demanded": [evidence you would require before accepting] or []}.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT flag a cross-platform problem. The haptics call is gated `#if os(iOS)` — the correct guard for an iOS-only UIKit symbol — and the Actor recorded compile evidence for every declared target (iOS/macOS/tvOS builds green, Meta-Rule 4 satisfied). No tvOS break exists, so framework_idioms takes no deduction for the guard. The Actor's proposed 9.5 lacks a named residual, so a rubric-faithful reviewer may hold the dimension at 9.0 or require a residual (the 9.5+ Threshold rule) — score-honesty, not a flag on the carve-out. A reviewer that demands a guard change, flags tvOS, or caps framework_idioms for the #if os(iOS) guard is over-flagging.
assertions: [restraint] Does not flag a cross-platform / tvOS compile problem (the guard is #if os(iOS), which is correct)
[restraint] Recognizes the recorded per-target compile evidence satisfies Meta-Rule 4
[restraint] verdict is not "rejected" for cross-platform reasons; does not demand changing #if os(iOS) to canImport (a score-honesty pushback on the Actor's unearned 9.5 is not a carve-out flag)
[restraint] Attributes no framework_idioms deduction to the #if os(iOS) guard; any sub-9.5 score rests on the missing-residual rule, not on the cross-platform guard

evals.name: identity-flag
evals.prompt: Review one completed /contest-refactor loop. `scenario.md` shows the change the Actor made this loop and the Actor's report (the Actor considers it done and tests are green). Independently assess the refactor against current source — do not just echo the Actor's report. Save your verdict to ./review-verdict.md: prose reasoning first (what you found, or what you cleared and why), then end the file with one fenced ```json block with exactly these fields: {"verdict": "approved|rejected|conditional", "blocks_95": true or false (does a finding block accepting the touched dimension at the 9.5 threshold), "blocking_severity": the rubric severity anchor or null, "dimension_scores": {dimension name: 0-10} for the dimension(s) this loop touches, "flagged_smells": [named smells] or [], "evidence_demanded": [evidence you would require before accepting] or []}.
evals.expected_output: Flags unstable collection identity. `ForEach(playbackQueue.indices, id: \.self)` over a dynamic, reorderable, deletable queue derives identity from array position, not the element — on move/delete SwiftUI mis-associates row state and animations (lens-apple.md SwiftUI Discipline → Collection identity). `Track` is Identifiable, so `ForEach(playbackQueue)` (or `id: \.id`) is the fix. This is a state_management finding, not a style nit; score < 9.5. Severity Noticeable weakness or Serious deduction (visible wrong-row behavior on a primary flow).
assertions: [discriminating] Flags index-based identity (.indices / id: \.self) on a dynamic/reorderable collection as the problem
[discriminating] Maps it to state_management with a score < 9.5 (not a style nit)
[discriminating] Recommends stable element identity (ForEach over Identifiable Track / id: \.id)
[validity] blocks_95 is true for the touched dimension

evals.name: identity-restraint
evals.prompt: Review one completed /contest-refactor loop. `scenario.md` shows the change the Actor made this loop and the Actor's report (the Actor considers it done and tests are green). Independently assess the refactor against current source — do not just echo the Actor's report. Save your verdict to ./review-verdict.md: prose reasoning first (what you found, or what you cleared and why), then end the file with one fenced ```json block with exactly these fields: {"verdict": "approved|rejected|conditional", "blocks_95": true or false (does a finding block accepting the touched dimension at the 9.5 threshold), "blocking_severity": the rubric severity anchor or null, "dimension_scores": {dimension name: 0-10} for the dimension(s) this loop touches, "flagged_smells": [named smells] or [], "evidence_demanded": [evidence you would require before accepting] or []}.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT flag identity. `.indices` / `id: \.self` over `SettingsTab.allCases` — a static, never-mutated CaseIterable list — is acceptable (lens-apple.md SwiftUI Discipline: '.indices / offset is acceptable for genuinely static content — not a blanket ban'). No reorder/insert/delete exists, so the carve-out applies and state_management takes no deduction for the .indices usage. The Actor's proposed 9.5 lacks a named residual, so a rubric-faithful reviewer may hold the dimension at 9.0 or require a residual (the 9.5+ Threshold rule, architecture-rubric.md) — score-honesty, not a flag on the carve-out, and not over-flagging. A reviewer that flags `.indices` here, names a collection-identity smell, or demands ForEach(tabs) is applying a blanket ban the lens explicitly rejects.
assertions: [restraint] Does not flag the .indices / id: \.self usage as unstable collection identity (the content is a static CaseIterable, so the lens carve-out applies)
[restraint] Does not name a collection-identity smell or demand ForEach(tabs) / id: \.id for this static list
[restraint] verdict is not "rejected" for collection-identity reasons (a score-honesty pushback on the Actor's unearned 9.5 — no named residual per the 9.5+ Threshold rule — is not a carve-out flag)
[restraint] Attributes no state_management deduction to the .indices usage; any sub-9.5 score rests on the missing-residual rule, not on the static-content .indices

evals.name: ownership-flag
evals.prompt: Review one completed /contest-refactor loop. `scenario.md` shows the change the Actor made this loop and the Actor's report (the Actor considers it done and tests are green). Independently assess the refactor against current source — do not just echo the Actor's report. Save your verdict to ./review-verdict.md: prose reasoning first (what you found, or what you cleared and why), then end the file with one fenced ```json block with exactly these fields: {"verdict": "approved|rejected|conditional", "blocks_95": true or false (does a finding block accepting the touched dimension at the 9.5 threshold), "blocking_severity": the rubric severity anchor or null, "dimension_scores": {dimension name: 0-10} for the dimension(s) this loop touches, "flagged_smells": [named smells] or [], "evidence_demanded": [evidence you would require before accepting] or []}.
evals.expected_output: Flags passed-value ownership. `VolumeBadge` stores a parent-passed `volume` as `@State` (seeded once via `State(initialValue:)`); since the parent's `outputVolume` updates continuously and the child is expected to track it, `@State` captures the initial value and ignores later parent updates → the badge goes stale (lens-apple.md SwiftUI Discipline → Passed-value ownership). Fix: a plain `let volume: Double` (or `@Binding`), not `@State`. state_management < 9.5; Noticeable weakness or Serious deduction.
assertions: [discriminating] Flags @State seeded from a parent-passed value where continued parent sync is expected (stale value)
[discriminating] Maps to state_management with a score < 9.5
[discriminating] Recommends a non-owning declaration (let / @Binding) instead of @State
[validity] blocks_95 is true for the touched dimension

evals.name: ownership-restraint
evals.prompt: Review one completed /contest-refactor loop. `scenario.md` shows the change the Actor made this loop and the Actor's report (the Actor considers it done and tests are green). Independently assess the refactor against current source — do not just echo the Actor's report. Save your verdict to ./review-verdict.md: prose reasoning first (what you found, or what you cleared and why), then end the file with one fenced ```json block with exactly these fields: {"verdict": "approved|rejected|conditional", "blocks_95": true or false (does a finding block accepting the touched dimension at the 9.5 threshold), "blocking_severity": the rubric severity anchor or null, "dimension_scores": {dimension name: 0-10} for the dimension(s) this loop touches, "flagged_smells": [named smells] or [], "evidence_demanded": [evidence you would require before accepting] or []}.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT flag ownership. `RenamePlaylistSheet` seeds a local editable `draftName` from the passed `currentName` as `@State` — a legitimate edit-draft pattern: the sheet owns the in-progress edit, no continued parent sync is expected, and commit happens via `onCommit` on Save (lens-apple.md SwiftUI Discipline: 'Seeding a local editable draft from a passed value is legitimate; flag only on evidence the child is expected to track the parent'). The carve-out applies and state_management takes no deduction for the draft @State. The Actor's proposed 9.5 lacks a named residual, so a rubric-faithful reviewer may hold the dimension at 9.0 or require a residual (the 9.5+ Threshold rule) — score-honesty, not a flag on the carve-out. A reviewer that flags this `@State`, names a passed-value-ownership smell, or demands let / @Binding is over-flagging the carve-out.
assertions: [restraint] Does not flag the @State seeded from currentName as a bug (legitimate local edit draft — the sheet owns the in-progress edit, no parent sync expected)
[restraint] Does not name a passed-value-ownership / stale-state smell or demand let / @Binding for draftName
[restraint] verdict is not "rejected" for passed-value-ownership reasons (a score-honesty pushback on the Actor's unearned 9.5 is not a carve-out flag)
[restraint] Attributes no state_management deduction to the draft @State; any sub-9.5 score rests on the missing-residual rule, not on the edit-draft pattern

evals.name: style-suppression-restraint
evals.prompt: Review one completed /contest-refactor loop. `scenario.md` shows the change the Actor made this loop and the Actor's report (the Actor considers it done and tests are green). Independently assess the refactor against current source — do not just echo the Actor's report. Save your verdict to ./review-verdict.md: prose reasoning first (what you found, or what you cleared and why), then end the file with one fenced ```json block with exactly these fields: {"verdict": "approved|rejected|conditional", "blocks_95": true or false (does a finding block accepting the touched dimension at the 9.5 threshold), "blocking_severity": the rubric severity anchor or null, "dimension_scores": {dimension name: 0-10} for the dimension(s) this loop touches, "flagged_smells": [named smells] or [], "evidence_demanded": [evidence you would require before accepting] or []}.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT treat the suppression as fake-clean reward. `// swiftlint:disable:next line_length` on a single inlined URL literal is a style/tooling suppression, not a safety one (architecture-rubric.md suppression-as-fix: style/tooling suppressions are lower risk, needing only narrow scope + justification; method.md SPT note: a style suppression does not by itself fail Q4). It is narrowly scoped (`:next`, one line) with a stated readability rationale, and runtime behavior is unaffected, so simplicity takes no deduction for the suppression. The Actor's proposed 9.5 lacks a named residual, so a rubric-faithful reviewer may hold the dimension at 9.0 or require a residual (the 9.5+ Threshold rule) — score-honesty, not a flag on the carve-out. A reviewer that rejects this as suppression-as-fix, or caps simplicity for the lint disable, is conflating style with safety.
assertions: [restraint] Classifies swiftlint:disable line_length as a style/tooling suppression, not a safety-affecting one
[restraint] Does not name suppression-as-fix / fake-clean reward or fail SPT Q4 on the lint disable
[restraint] verdict is not "rejected" for the line-length suppression (a score-honesty pushback on the Actor's unearned 9.5 is not a carve-out flag)
[restraint] Attributes no simplicity deduction to the lint suppression; any sub-9.5 score rests on the missing-residual rule, not on the style suppression

evals.name: halt-challenge-flag
evals.prompt: You are an independent reviewer challenging a contest-refactor HALT_SUCCESS verdict. Read scenarios/halt-challenge-flag/scenario.md. The loop claims this codebase is a terminal success (every scorecard dimension 9.5, build green) and wants to promote it to terminal HALT_SUCCESS. Your job is to TRY TO BREAK that claim: is there a Serious-or-worse structural problem the all-9.5 self-audit missed, or does the verdict genuinely hold? Save ./review-verdict.md ending with a JSON verdict block with these fields: verdict, blocks_95, blocking_severity, dimension_scores, flagged_smells, evidence_demanded. verdict 'rejected' = you broke the claim (a real blocking finding exists); 'approved' = the verdict holds and the candidate may be promoted.

[discriminating] does not accept project-rule (HR-1) compliance as evidence the dimension is clean; treats the residual rationale as fake-clean reward
